entry.rationale: Death-proximate records (death certificate, burial register, probate inventory/will) are the logical starting point because they are closest in time to her death, may directly name parents or birth county, and anchor identity before building on Ohio and Pennsylvania searches. Wisconsin became a state in 1848 and early vital records, county court probate files, and church burial registers exist for Green County. This is the highest-yield gatekeeper step.

entry.rationale: Wisconsin Death Index, 1820-1907 (collection 1940759) is the fastest free indexed search. Although systematic state death registration began in 1852, this index draws on county clerk records and compiled sources and may capture pre-1852 entries for Green County. A hit would confirm Christiana's death date and location and possibly name informants.

Wisconsin Deaths and Burials, 1835-1968 (collection 1708699) is a complementary compiled index that incorporates church burial registers and cemetery transcriptions. It may contain entries not in the death index (collection 1940759), particularly for pre-registration deaths from religious communities in Monroe.

Search Find a Grave and the Wisconsin Gravestone Photo Project (wisconsingravestones.org) for Christiana Clark in Green County. Gravestones from this era sometimes record birthplace, age at death, or parents' names. Cemetery transcriptions are free and may also confirm a burial companion (William Clark) whose death record could then be searched for family information.

Wisconsin Probate Estate Files, 1848-1948 (collection 1874190) holds Green County probate packet images from the year of statehood. County probate courts were organized in 1848. Search for both Christiana Clark (she may have had an estate if she held property) and William Clark (if he survived her, his file would name heirs and could identify Christiana's origins or parents; if he predeceased her, her file would be the target). Probate inventories, petitions, and guardian bonds name relatives and frequently state birthplaces.

Ancestry: Wisconsin Wills and Probate Records, 1800-1987 (collection 9088) — paid fallback for the FamilySearch probate search (pli_004). May have an index or different image set that makes it easier to locate Clark estate packets within the browse-only FamilySearch collection.

MyHeritage Wisconsin Death Index, 1808-1909 — a third compiled death index sourced independently of FamilySearch collections. Useful if pli_001 and pli_002 return nothing; different source transcriptions sometimes catch entries missed elsewhere.

A match here would be direct evidence naming both parents.

For a parentage question, planning standards require a dedicated search for the candidate parents' marriage to each other. The parents' marriage record in Berks County's German Reformed or Lutheran registers would supply the mother's maiden name — information census and death records almost never carry. FamilySearch collection 1681011 'Pennsylvania, Marriages, 1709-1940' (661,399 records) covers this jurisdiction. A Christiana born 2 March 1783 would have parents who married roughly 1778-1784.

entry.rationale: Identity-bridge priority: if Adam Schreck left a will in Berks County naming his daughter Christiana (later Clark), that is the strongest possible independent link between the Tulpehocken Christina Schreck (baptized 1783) and the Wisconsin Christiana Clark (died 1848). Adam Schreck was father of a child born 1783; if he was born ~1750-1760 he could have died 1800-1840. Pennsylvania Wills and Probate Records, 1683-1993 are indexed on FamilySearch. A will naming a daughter Christiana Clark would close the identity gap the mentor flagged.

FAN/sibling approach: searching the same HSP baptism collection (4138679) for other children of Adam Schreck (fatherGivenName Adam, fatherSurname Schreck, Berks County, 1770-1800) will establish the Schreck sibling group. If multiple children of Adam Schreck and Margreth are documented in Berks County, and one of them is Christina/Christiana later identified as Christiana Clark, the family group corroborates the identity claim. Siblings also provide context for resolving whether Christiana/Christina is the same person as the Wisconsin decedent.

Marriage record for William Clark and Christiana/Christina would be a direct identity anchor: a Pennsylvania marriage record naming the bride as Christina Schreck (or daughter of Adam Schreck) marrying William Clark would close the Schreck-to-Clark gap definitively. If they married in Berks County (where the Schrecks lived) or an adjacent county before moving to Ohio, the record would be in FamilySearch collection 1681011 (Pennsylvania Marriages 1709-1940). Searching William Clark + Christiana/Christina, PA, 1800-1820.

Ohio census records (1820, 1830, 1840) for William Clark in Coshocton County establish the household and confirm a woman of approximately Christiana's age (born 1783: age ~37 in 1820, ~47 in 1830, ~57 in 1840) in the household. While pre-1850 censuses do not name individuals other than the head, age-bracket confirmation together with the family's documented residence in Coshocton County supports the identity argument as corroborating circumstantial evidence.
